data_table.Clinical details: Gestation: 32 weeks (confirmed by antenatal records); Uterine fundal height: At the umbilicus, consistent with gestation; Time since arrest: 4 minutes; CPR quality: High quality with manual uterine displacement; Rhythm: PEA throughout; Reversible causes addressed: IV access, adrenaline given, no obvious cause identified

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue CPR with manual left uterine displacement for a further 6 minutes to complete a standard 10-minute resuscitation cycle before considering any surgical intervention for the arrest
options.rationale: Delaying perimortem caesarean section beyond 4-5 minutes of unsuccessful CPR reduces both maternal and neonatal survival. The gravid uterus at 32 weeks causes significant aortocaval compression that impedes venous return and reduces cardiac output even with manual uterine displacement and high-quality CPR. Perimortem caesarean section is a resuscitative procedure that improves maternal haemodynamics by relieving aortocaval compression, not just a procedure to save the foetus. Waiting 10 minutes significantly worsens outcomes. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.10)

options.text: Place the patient in a full left lateral position for CPR to relieve aortocaval compression, as this is more effective than manual uterine displacement, and continue resuscitation for a further 10 minutes
options.rationale: Full left lateral positioning makes effective chest compressions extremely difficult and is not recommended during CPR. Manual uterine displacement (already being performed) is the recommended method for relieving aortocaval compression during resuscitation, as it allows the patient to remain supine for optimal chest compressions. Furthermore, after 4 minutes of CPR without ROSC at 32 weeks gestation, perimortem caesarean section should be initiated rather than continuing CPR with positional changes alone. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.10)

options.text: Transfer the patient urgently to the operating theatre for perimortem caesarean section under optimal surgical conditions, continuing CPR during transfer to ensure the best possible surgical environment
options.rationale: Perimortem caesarean section should be performed at the site of the arrest (in this case, the emergency department) rather than delaying for transfer to an operating theatre. The critical time window is delivery within 4-5 minutes of cardiac arrest, and any transfer time reduces the chance of both maternal and neonatal survival. The procedure should be performed wherever the patient is located, using whatever equipment is available. CPR must continue throughout the procedure. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.10)

options.text: Consult the on-call obstetrician to attend and perform the perimortem caesarean section while continuing CPR, as the procedure requires an experienced obstetrician to minimise maternal surgical complications
options.rationale: While an obstetrician should be called urgently, waiting for their arrival is inappropriate if it delays the procedure beyond the 4-5 minute window. Perimortem caesarean section is a resuscitative procedure that any doctor trained in the technique can perform in an emergency. The procedure should be initiated immediately by the most experienced available clinician - this may be the emergency physician already present. Time is the critical factor, not the specialty of the operator. CPR continues throughout. (Source: RANZCOG maternal collapse guideline)

options.text: Commence perimortem caesarean section immediately at the current location while continuing CPR, aiming for delivery within 1 minute of skin incision, performed by the most experienced clinician available
options.rationale: At 4 minutes of cardiac arrest without ROSC in a 32-week pregnancy, perimortem caesarean section should be commenced immediately. ANZCOR guidelines recommend that the procedure should aim for delivery within 4-5 minutes of cardiac arrest onset. It should be performed at the site of arrest by the most experienced available clinician - in this scenario, the emergency physician if no obstetrician is immediately present. CPR must continue throughout the procedure. The aim is a rapid midline incision and classical caesarean incision for fastest delivery. The procedure benefits the mother by relieving aortocaval compression and improving the effectiveness of CPR, in addition to giving the foetus the best chance of intact survival. Manual uterine displacement, while helpful, cannot fully relieve the compression caused by a 32-week gravid uterus. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.10)

explanation.summary: Perimortem caesarean section is a resuscitative procedure performed during maternal cardiac arrest when CPR has not achieved return of spontaneous circulation within 4-5 minutes. It serves a dual purpose: improving maternal resuscitation by relieving aortocaval compression from the gravid uterus (significant from 20 weeks gestation), and providing the foetus the best chance of survival. The procedure should be performed at the site of arrest by the most experienced available clinician, without delay for transfer to an operating theatre or arrival of a specialist obstetrician. A midline abdominal incision and classical uterine incision are used for speed. CPR must continue throughout the procedure and after delivery.
key_points: Perimortem caesarean section should aim for delivery within 4-5 minutes of maternal cardiac arrest if CPR has not achieved ROSC
The procedure is performed at the site of arrest by the most experienced available clinician - do not delay for theatre transfer or specialist attendance
Manual left uterine displacement is used during CPR rather than left lateral tilt, as the supine position is essential for effective chest compressions
explanation.pearls: Perimortem caesarean section should aim for delivery within 5 minutes of cardiac arrest onset - it is a maternal resuscitative procedure that improves CPR efficacy by relieving aortocaval compression, not solely a foetal rescue procedure.

sources.label: Queensland Health Maternal Cardiac Arrest guideline

stem: A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of dysuria, fevers, and rigors. He has a background of type 2 diabetes mellitus and benign prostatic hyperplasia. On arrival he is confused and appears unwell.

data_table.Vitals: Temperature: 39.4 C; Heart rate: 118 bpm; Blood pressure: 88/52 mmHg; Respiratory rate: 24 breaths/min; SpO2: 96% on room air
data_table.Investigations: White cell count: 18.2 x10^9/L; Creatinine: 210 micromol/L (baseline 95); Lactate: 4.2 mmol/L; Urinalysis: Leucocytes 3+, nitrites positive

lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic regimen to commence within the first hour?

options.text: Gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV and ampicillin 2 g IV
options.rationale: This patient has sepsis secondary to a urinary source with haemodynamic compromise. The eTG Antibiotic recommends gentamicin plus ampicillin as empiric therapy for severe urosepsis, providing gram-negative and enterococcal coverage. Blood cultures must be obtained before antibiotic administration, and 30 mL/kg crystalloid should be commenced for hypotension as part of the hour-1 bundle. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Ceftriaxone 1 g IV and metronidazole 500 mg IV
options.rationale: This combination is more appropriate for intra-abdominal sepsis rather than urosepsis. Metronidazole provides anaerobic coverage that is not required for a urinary tract source. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV as monotherapy
options.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam provides broad-spectrum coverage but is not recommended as first-line empiric therapy for urosepsis in Australian guidelines. It may be considered in specific situations such as known resistant organisms or healthcare-associated infections. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 1.2 g IV as monotherapy
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate may be used for less severe urinary infections but provides insufficient coverage for severe urosepsis with septic shock physiology. It lacks reliable activity against many gram-negative organisms causing complicated UTIs. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV and vancomycin 25 mg/kg IV
options.rationale: Fluoroquinolones are not first-line for urosepsis in Australian guidelines due to resistance concerns and adverse effects. Vancomycin adds unnecessary MRSA coverage when there is no clinical indication for it in this presentation. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: This patient presents with sepsis secondary to a urinary tract source, meeting qSOFA criteria (altered mental status, systolic BP less than 100 mmHg, respiratory rate 22 or more). The hour-1 sepsis bundle requires blood cultures before antibiotics, empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension, and lactate measurement. For severe urosepsis, eTG Antibiotic recommends gentamicin plus ampicillin to cover gram-negative organisms and enterococci. The elevated lactate of 4.2 mmol/L indicates tissue hypoperfusion and should prompt aggressive resuscitation.
key_points: qSOFA score of 3 (confusion, hypotension, tachypnoea) identifies high-risk sepsis requiring urgent intervention
Hour-1 bundle: blood cultures before antibiotics, empiric antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, lactate measurement
Gentamicin plus ampicillin is the recommended empiric regimen for severe urosepsis per eTG Antibiotic
explanation.pearls: Gentamicin dosing in sepsis is 5 mg/kg IV (ideal body weight) with monitoring of renal function and drug levels. Lactate greater than 4 mmol/L is associated with significantly increased mortality and mandates aggressive resuscitation within 1 hour.

stem: A 58-year-old woman is in the emergency department resuscitation bay with septic shock secondary to community-acquired pneumonia. She has received appropriate empiric antibiotics. She has now received 30 mL/kg of balanced crystalloid over the past 45 minutes. Her indwelling catheter shows urine output of 10 mL over the last hour.

data_table.Vitals post fluid: Temperature: 38.8 C; Heart rate: 124 bpm; Blood pressure: 78/48 mmHg; MAP: 58 mmHg; Respiratory rate: 28 breaths/min; SpO2: 92% on 10 L/min via Hudson mask
data_table.Investigations: Lactate (repeat): 5.8 mmol/L (was 4.1 mmol/L at presentation); pH: 7.24; Bicarbonate: 16 mmol/L

lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer a further 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus over 30 minutes
options.rationale: The patient has already received the recommended initial 30 mL/kg crystalloid resuscitation and remains hypotensive with a rising lactate. Further large-volume fluid boluses without vasopressor support risk fluid overload and worsening pulmonary oedema, particularly in the context of her declining oxygenation. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Commence noradrenaline infusion targeting MAP of 65 mmHg or greater
options.rationale: Noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor for septic shock refractory to initial crystalloid resuscitation. It should be commenced when hypotension persists despite 30 mL/kg fluid resuscitation, targeting a MAP of 65 mmHg or greater. It can be started via peripheral IV while central venous access is being arranged. The rising lactate and metabolic acidosis indicate worsening tissue hypoperfusion requiring urgent haemodynamic support. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Commence adrenaline infusion as it also provides inotropic support
options.rationale: Adrenaline is not the recommended first-line vasopressor in septic shock. While it provides both vasopressor and inotropic effects, it is associated with greater tachycardia and metabolic side effects including lactic acidosis, which complicates lactate monitoring. It may be considered as a second-line agent or when additional inotropic support is needed. (Source: ANZICS)

options.text: Administer hydrocortisone 50 mg IV 6-hourly as first-line intervention
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone 200 mg/day (50 mg 6-hourly) is recommended for septic shock that remains refractory despite adequate fluid resuscitation and vasopressor therapy. It should not be commenced before a vasopressor trial, as it is an adjunctive measure for vasopressor-refractory shock rather than a first-line haemodynamic agent. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Commence vasopressin infusion as the preferred initial vasopressor
options.rationale: Vasopressin is a second-line vasopressor in septic shock. It may be added to noradrenaline to reduce catecholamine requirements or as an alternative if arrhythmias develop, but it is not recommended as the initial vasopressor ahead of noradrenaline per current guidelines. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

explanation.summary: This patient has fluid-refractory septic shock with persistent hypotension (MAP 58 mmHg), rising lactate, metabolic acidosis, oliguria, and worsening oxygenation despite 30 mL/kg crystalloid. The next step is initiation of noradrenaline as the first-line vasopressor, targeting a MAP of 65 mmHg or greater. Noradrenaline can be commenced via a peripheral intravenous line while central access is established. Hydrocortisone at 200 mg/day should be considered if the patient remains vasopressor-dependent despite adequate doses. ICU referral should occur early in the resuscitation process.
key_points: Noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor for fluid-refractory septic shock with a MAP target of 65 mmHg or greater
Vasopressors should not be delayed for central venous access - peripheral administration is acceptable as a bridge
Hydrocortisone 200 mg/day is reserved for vasopressor-refractory shock, not as a first-line haemodynamic agent
explanation.pearls: Noradrenaline can be safely administered via a peripheral IV cannula at concentrations up to 8 mcg/mL for initial resuscitation. Hydrocortisone 50 mg IV 6-hourly (total 200 mg/day) should be considered when noradrenaline requirements exceed 0.25 mcg/kg/min.

stem: A 28-year-old previously well woman presents to the emergency department with a 12-hour history of severe headache, fever, and neck stiffness. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. She is not immunocompromised. On examination she has photophobia and a positive Kernig sign. There are no focal neurological deficits, her pupils are equal and reactive, and her GCS is 15.

data_table.Vitals: Temperature: 39.2 C; Heart rate: 108 bpm; Blood pressure: 132/78 mmHg; Respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min; GCS: 15 (E4 V5 M6)

lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: CT brain followed by lumbar puncture, then commence antibiotics based on CSF results
options.rationale: Delaying antibiotics until imaging and CSF results are available is inappropriate in suspected bacterial meningitis. In this patient with no indications for CT before LP (no focal deficits, GCS 15, immunocompetent, no new seizures), antibiotics should be given as soon as possible. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Lumbar puncture, then ceftriaxone 2 g IV BD once CSF confirms bacterial meningitis
options.rationale: While LP can proceed without CT in this patient, waiting for CSF confirmation before commencing antibiotics is inappropriate. Empiric antibiotics should be administered as soon as possible - ideally within 60 minutes of presentation. Delays in antibiotic administration are associated with increased mortality. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Dexamethasone 10 mg IV, then ceftriaxone 2 g IV, and proceed to lumbar puncture
options.rationale: In suspected bacterial meningitis with no contraindications to LP, dexamethasone 10 mg IV should be given before or with the first dose of antibiotics, followed by ceftriaxone 2 g IV BD. Dexamethasone reduces mortality and neurological sequelae, particularly in pneumococcal meningitis. This patient has no indications for CT before LP (GCS 15, no focal deficits, immunocompetent, no new seizures), so LP can proceed after treatment is initiated. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV BD and benzylpenicillin 2.4 g IV 4-hourly without dexamethasone
options.rationale: Adding benzylpenicillin to ceftriaxone is unnecessary in a young immunocompetent patient. Benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin is added for Listeria coverage in patients older than 50, immunocompromised, or pregnant. Omitting dexamethasone is also incorrect, as adjunctive dexamethasone is recommended in suspected bacterial meningitis. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: CT brain first to exclude raised intracranial pressure, then lumbar puncture and antibiotics
options.rationale: CT before LP is indicated when there are focal neurological deficits, GCS less than 12, immunocompromise, or new-onset seizures. This patient has none of these features, so CT is not required before LP. Importantly, even when CT is indicated, antibiotics should not be delayed pending imaging. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: This young immunocompetent woman presents with classic features of bacterial meningitis. She has no indications for CT before LP (GCS 15, no focal deficits, not immunocompromised, no seizures). The priority is dexamethasone 10 mg IV given before or with the first antibiotic dose, followed by ceftriaxone 2 g IV BD. Dexamethasone should be continued at 10 mg IV 6-hourly for 4 days if pneumococcal meningitis is confirmed. Lumbar puncture should be performed promptly but must not delay antibiotic administration.
key_points: Dexamethasone 10 mg IV must be given before or with the first dose of antibiotics to reduce mortality and neurological sequelae
CT before LP is only indicated for focal neurology, GCS less than 12, immunocompromise, or new seizures
Empiric ceftriaxone 2 g IV BD is standard for immunocompetent adults aged 18 to 50 with suspected bacterial meningitis
explanation.pearls: Dexamethasone 10 mg IV 6-hourly for 4 days should be continued if Streptococcus pneumoniae is identified but can be ceased if an alternative organism is confirmed. Listeria coverage with benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin is added for patients over 50 years, pregnant, or immunocompromised.

stem: A 45-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his partner witnessed a generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home. Over the preceding 3 days he has had fevers, increasing confusion, and unusual behaviour including attempting to leave the house undressed. He has no prior history of seizures and takes no medications. On arrival he is drowsy but rousable, with a temperature of 39.1 C. He has no neck stiffness and no rash. A CT brain without contrast is reported as normal.

data_table.CSF results: Opening pressure: 18 cmH2O; White cells: 85 x10^6/L (95% lymphocytes); Protein: 1.2 g/L; Glucose: 3.4 mmol/L (serum glucose 5.8 mmol/L); Gram stain: No organisms seen

lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence ceftriaxone 2 g IV BD and await CSF bacterial culture results
options.rationale: While ceftriaxone covers bacterial meningitis, the CSF profile here shows lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein and relatively preserved glucose, which is characteristic of viral rather than bacterial aetiology. The clinical presentation with behavioural change, seizures, and fever strongly suggests HSV encephalitis, which requires IV aciclovir. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Request an MRI brain before commencing any antimicrobial therapy
options.rationale: While MRI is important for diagnosis (temporal lobe signal abnormality supports HSV encephalitis), treatment must not be delayed pending imaging. IV aciclovir should be commenced empirically as soon as HSV encephalitis is suspected, given the significant morbidity and mortality associated with treatment delays. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Await CSF herpes simplex virus PCR result before starting antiviral therapy
options.rationale: CSF HSV PCR is the confirmatory test but results are not available immediately. Delaying aciclovir for PCR confirmation is inappropriate because untreated HSV encephalitis has mortality exceeding 70%. Empiric aciclovir must be commenced on clinical suspicion alone. A negative early PCR does not exclude HSV encephalitis and may need repeating. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence aciclovir 10 mg/kg IV 8-hourly and arrange MRI brain with contrast
options.rationale: This presentation of fever, behavioural change, seizures, and CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein and preserved glucose is highly suspicious for HSV encephalitis. Aciclovir 10 mg/kg IV 8-hourly must be commenced empirically without delay. MRI brain will typically show temporal lobe signal abnormality and should be arranged but must not delay treatment. CSF HSV PCR should be sent for confirmation. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence valaciclovir 1 g orally 8-hourly as this has equivalent bioavailability
options.rationale: Oral valaciclovir is not appropriate for the treatment of HSV encephalitis. Intravenous aciclovir at 10 mg/kg 8-hourly is required to achieve adequate CNS drug concentrations. Oral valaciclovir is used for less severe herpes simplex infections such as genital herpes or herpes zoster. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: This patient presents with the classic triad of HSV encephalitis: fever, altered behaviour or personality change, and seizures with temporal lobe predilection. The CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein and relatively preserved glucose, consistent with a viral aetiology. IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg 8-hourly must be started empirically as soon as HSV encephalitis is suspected, as delays in treatment significantly worsen outcomes. MRI brain typically shows temporal lobe signal abnormality on T2/FLAIR sequences. CSF HSV PCR is the gold standard diagnostic test but must not delay empiric treatment.
key_points: HSV encephalitis classically presents with fever, personality change, temporal lobe seizures, and confusion
IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg 8-hourly must be commenced empirically and not delayed for investigations
CSF in viral encephalitis shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, and preserved or mildly low glucose
explanation.pearls: Aciclovir 10 mg/kg IV 8-hourly is continued for 14 to 21 days for confirmed HSV encephalitis. Adequate IV hydration is essential to prevent aciclovir-induced crystalline nephropathy. CSF HSV PCR may be negative in the first 72 hours and should be repeated if clinical suspicion remains high.

stem: A 34-year-old woman presents to general practice requesting better management of her migraines. She has been experiencing migraine with aura approximately 6 days per month for the past year. She uses sumatriptan 50 mg for acute episodes with good relief but is concerned about increasing frequency. She has a history of asthma managed with salbutamol as needed. She takes a combined oral contraceptive pill. Her blood pressure is 118/72 mmHg.
lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate preventive medication to recommend?

options.text: Propranolol 40 mg orally twice daily as a first-line preventive agent
options.rationale: Propranolol is a first-line migraine preventive agent, but it is contraindicated in this patient due to her history of asthma. Beta-blockers can precipitate bronchospasm and should be avoided in patients with asthma. (Source: eTG Neurology)

options.text: Sumatriptan 50 mg orally daily as a preventive rather than acute therapy
options.rationale: Triptans are acute migraine treatments and are not indicated for prevention. Daily triptan use risks medication overuse headache when used more than 10 days per month. Triptans should be reserved for acute episodic treatment. (Source: Headache Australia)

options.text: Topiramate 25 mg orally daily, titrating up as tolerated over several weeks
options.rationale: Topiramate is an effective migraine preventive agent but requires specific counselling regarding teratogenicity. It interacts with the combined oral contraceptive pill by reducing its efficacy through hepatic enzyme induction. While it could be considered, the interaction with her current contraception and the availability of safer alternatives make it less appropriate as first choice. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Verapamil 240 mg orally daily in a modified-release preparation
options.rationale: Verapamil is the first-line preventive agent for cluster headache, not migraine. It is not recommended as a standard preventive therapy for migraine in Australian guidelines. Appropriate first-line migraine preventive agents include propranolol, amitriptyline, and candesartan. (Source: eTG Neurology)

options.text: Amitriptyline 10 mg orally at night, titrating to 25 to 50 mg as tolerated
options.rationale: Amitriptyline is a first-line migraine preventive agent that is appropriate for this patient. It avoids the beta-blocker contraindication due to asthma and the drug interaction concerns with topiramate and the oral contraceptive pill. It is started at a low dose of 10 mg at night and titrated upwards. Common side effects include drowsiness and dry mouth. (Source: eTG Neurology)

explanation.summary: Migraine prevention should be considered when attacks occur 4 or more days per month, when acute treatments are ineffective, or when there is significant disability. First-line preventive agents in Australia include propranolol, amitriptyline, and candesartan. This patient has asthma, which contraindicates propranolol, and is taking the combined oral contraceptive pill, which interacts with topiramate. Amitriptyline is appropriate as it avoids both these issues. Notably, this patient also has migraine with aura, which is itself a reason to reconsider the combined oral contraceptive pill due to increased stroke risk.
key_points: Migraine prevention is indicated when attacks occur 4 or more days per month or cause significant disability
Beta-blockers are contraindicated in patients with asthma due to risk of bronchospasm
Triptans are acute treatments only and should not be used for migraine prevention
explanation.pearls: Amitriptyline for migraine prevention is started at 10 mg at night and titrated to a typical dose of 25 to 50 mg over several weeks. A preventive trial should be continued for at least 2 to 3 months at adequate dose before assessing efficacy. Migraine with aura combined with the oral contraceptive pill increases ischaemic stroke risk.

stem: A 38-year-old man presents to the emergency department at 2 am with excruciating pain around his right eye that began abruptly 30 minutes ago. He is pacing the room and unable to sit still. He reports he has had similar episodes nightly for the past 3 weeks, each lasting approximately 45 to 90 minutes. He has a watering right eye and his right nostril is blocked. The episodes resolve spontaneously but are severely disabling. He has no significant past medical history. Neurological examination is non-focal between attacks.

data_table.Examination: Right eye: Conjunctival injection, lacrimation; Right pupil: Miotic compared to left; Right nostril: Congested; Right forehead: Mild diaphoresis

lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate acute treatment for this presentation?

options.text: High-flow oxygen 12 to 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15 to 20 minutes
options.rationale: This patient presents with cluster headache - severe unilateral periorbital pain with ipsilateral autonomic features (conjunctival injection, lacrimation, nasal congestion, miosis, forehead diaphoresis) and restless agitation. High-flow oxygen at 12 to 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask is first-line acute treatment in the emergency department, providing relief within 15 minutes in approximately 70% of patients. It is safe and has no significant contraindications. (Source: Headache Australia)

options.text: Oral sumatriptan 100 mg with an anti-emetic for suspected severe migraine
options.rationale: This is cluster headache, not migraine. Cluster headache is distinguished by strictly unilateral pain with prominent ipsilateral cranial autonomic features and restless agitation rather than the quiet retreat seen in migraine. Oral sumatriptan has too slow an onset for cluster headache attacks. If sumatriptan is used for cluster headache, the subcutaneous route at 6 mg is preferred. (Source: eTG Neurology)

options.text: Carbamazepine 200 mg orally for suspected trigeminal neuralgia
options.rationale: Trigeminal neuralgia causes brief lancinating pain lasting seconds, typically triggered by light touch or eating, without autonomic features. This patient has prolonged attacks lasting 45 to 90 minutes with prominent autonomic features, which is characteristic of cluster headache rather than trigeminal neuralgia. Carbamazepine is the first-line treatment for trigeminal neuralgia. (Source: eTG Neurology)

options.text: Verapamil 80 mg orally three times daily as first-line treatment for this condition
options.rationale: Verapamil is the first-line preventive therapy for cluster headache, not an acute treatment. It takes days to weeks to become effective and requires ECG monitoring for PR prolongation and heart block at each dose titration. While it should be initiated in this patient for prevention, it will not address the current acute attack. (Source: Headache Australia)

options.text: Ibuprofen 400 mg orally with paracetamol 1 g for acute pain relief
options.rationale: Simple oral analgesics including NSAIDs and paracetamol are ineffective for cluster headache attacks. The rapid onset and severity of cluster headache pain requires fast-acting treatments such as high-flow oxygen or subcutaneous sumatriptan. Oral analgesics are too slow in onset to be useful given typical attack durations. (Source: Headache Australia)

explanation.summary: Cluster headache is characterised by severe unilateral periorbital or temporal pain lasting 15 to 180 minutes, occurring in bouts (clusters) of weeks to months. Attacks are accompanied by ipsilateral cranial autonomic features including conjunctival injection, lacrimation, nasal congestion, rhinorrhoea, miosis, ptosis, and facial sweating. Patients are typically agitated and restless during attacks, unlike migraine sufferers who prefer to lie still. Acute treatment consists of high-flow oxygen at 12 to 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask and/or subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg. Preventive therapy with verapamil should be commenced early in a cluster bout.
key_points: Cluster headache is distinguished by strictly unilateral pain with ipsilateral autonomic features and restless agitation
High-flow oxygen 12 to 15 L/min via non-rebreather and subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg are first-line acute treatments
Verapamil is the first-line preventive agent and requires ECG monitoring for heart block at each dose titration
explanation.pearls: Subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg provides relief within 15 minutes in approximately 75% of cluster headache attacks. Verapamil is titrated from 80 mg three times daily up to 960 mg/day with ECG performed before each dose increase to monitor for PR prolongation exceeding 200 milliseconds.

stem:
A 26-year-old woman presents with a 6-week history of progressive daily headaches that are worse in the morning and when bending forward. She reports intermittent episodes of brief visual loss lasting seconds, particularly when changing posture, and a pulsatile whooshing sound in her left ear. Her BMI is 38 kg/m2. She commenced a combined oral contraceptive pill 4 months ago.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilloedema with haemorrhages. Visual acuity is 6/9 bilaterally, and perimetry shows enlarged blind spots. MRI brain with MR venography shows no intracranial mass lesion and patent venous sinuses.

data_table.Lumbar puncture: Opening pressure: 34 cmH2O; White cells: < 5 x10^6/L; Protein: 0.28 g/L; Glucose: 3.6 mmol/L (serum 5.2 mmol/L)

lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management plan?

options.text: Commence topiramate 25 mg daily as it also assists weight loss efforts
options.rationale: While topiramate has weak carbonic anhydrase inhibitor activity and may promote weight loss, it is not the first-line pharmacological treatment for idiopathic intracranial hypertension. Acetazolamide is the established first-line agent with better evidence for reducing CSF pressure and improving visual outcomes. Topiramate may be considered if acetazolamide is not tolerated. (Source: eTG Neurology)

options.text: Urgent optic nerve sheath fenestration given the visual field abnormalities
options.rationale: Optic nerve sheath fenestration is reserved for rapidly progressive or severe visual loss that is not responding to medical therapy, or as a temporising measure in fulminant disease. This patient has mildly reduced acuity and enlarged blind spots without evidence of rapidly progressive visual loss, so surgical intervention is not indicated initially. Medical management should be trialled first. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Acetazolamide 250 mg orally BD, cease the OCP, and counsel regarding weight loss
options.rationale: This patient meets diagnostic criteria for idiopathic intracranial hypertension: papilloedema, elevated opening pressure (greater than 25 cmH2O), normal CSF composition, normal MRI with no venous sinus thrombosis, and no alternative diagnosis. Initial management includes acetazolamide 250 mg BD (titrated up as needed to a maximum of 4 g/day), cessation of the combined oral contraceptive pill which may exacerbate the condition, and sustained weight loss of 5 to 10% which is the only intervention shown to achieve long-term remission. Regular ophthalmological review is essential. (Source: eTG Neurology)

options.text: Furosemide 40 mg daily and serial lumbar punctures every 2 weeks
options.rationale: Furosemide is not first-line therapy for idiopathic intracranial hypertension. Serial therapeutic lumbar punctures provide only transient relief and are not a sustainable management strategy. CSF is regenerated within hours, so the benefit is short-lived. Acetazolamide is the first-line pharmacological agent. (Source: eTG Neurology)

options.text: Commence dexamethasone 4 mg IV BD to reduce intracranial pressure urgently
options.rationale: Corticosteroids may provide short-term reduction in intracranial pressure but are not appropriate for ongoing management of idiopathic intracranial hypertension. Chronic corticosteroid use exacerbates weight gain and can paradoxically worsen the condition on withdrawal. They may be considered as a very short-term bridge in fulminant disease. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

explanation.summary: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension predominantly affects young women with obesity and is characterised by raised intracranial pressure without a structural cause. Diagnosis requires papilloedema, elevated LP opening pressure (greater than 25 cmH2O in adults), normal CSF composition, and exclusion of secondary causes including cerebral venous sinus thrombosis on MR venography. Key symptoms include progressive headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, and visual field deficits. The combined oral contraceptive pill is a recognised exacerbating factor and should be ceased. Management centres on acetazolamide, weight loss, and regular visual monitoring.
key_points: Diagnosis requires papilloedema, LP opening pressure greater than 25 cmH2O, normal CSF composition, and exclusion of venous sinus thrombosis
Acetazolamide 250 mg BD titrated to response is first-line pharmacotherapy, with sustained weight loss of 5 to 10% for long-term remission
Surgical intervention (optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF shunting) is reserved for rapidly progressive visual loss refractory to medical therapy
explanation.pearls: Acetazolamide is started at 250 mg BD and can be titrated to a maximum of 4 g/day. Patients should be warned about paraesthesiae, dysgeusia, and the risk of renal calculi. MR venography is essential to exclude cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, which mimics idiopathic intracranial hypertension but requires anticoagulation.

stem: A 30-year-old man presents with a 3-day history of chest discomfort, progressive dyspnoea on exertion, and palpitations. He had a febrile illness with myalgia 10 days ago. He has no cardiac history and takes no medications. On examination he has an elevated JVP, a third heart sound, and bibasal crepitations. His ECG shows sinus tachycardia with diffuse T-wave inversion across the precordial leads.

data_table.Vitals: Temperature: 37.2 C; Heart rate: 112 bpm; Blood pressure: 102/68 mmHg; Respiratory rate: 22 breaths/min; SpO2: 94% on room air
data_table.Investigations: Troponin T: 482 ng/L (reference < 14); NT-proBNP: 2850 pg/mL (reference < 125); CRP: 64 mg/L; Echocardiogram: Dilated LV, LVEF 30%, global hypokinesis, trace pericardial effusion

lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate management approach?

options.text: Ibuprofen 600 mg TDS and colchicine 500 mcg BD as for acute pericarditis
options.rationale: NSAIDs should be avoided in myocarditis with left ventricular dysfunction. While pericarditis and myocarditis may coexist, NSAIDs can worsen heart failure by promoting sodium and fluid retention and may increase myocardial damage in animal models of myocarditis. The predominant pathology here is myocarditis given the significant troponin elevation and reduced LVEF. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular)

options.text: Heart failure therapy with ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker, exercise restriction, and cardiac MRI
options.rationale: This patient has acute myocarditis with significant left ventricular systolic dysfunction (EF 30%). Management includes guideline-directed heart failure therapy (ACE inhibitor or ARB and careful introduction of beta-blocker once stable), strict exercise restriction for 3 to 6 months, continuous cardiac monitoring for arrhythmias, and cardiac MRI for diagnostic confirmation showing myocardial oedema and late gadolinium enhancement. NSAIDs are contraindicated with LV dysfunction. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular)

options.text: Urgent coronary angiography to exclude acute coronary syndrome as the primary diagnosis
options.rationale: While the troponin is markedly elevated, the clinical context of a preceding viral illness in a young patient with global hypokinesis on echo (rather than regional wall motion abnormality) strongly suggests myocarditis. Coronary artery disease is unlikely in a 30-year-old without risk factors. Cardiac MRI is the preferred confirmatory investigation for myocarditis. Coronary angiography may be considered if the diagnosis remains uncertain. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular)

options.text: Intravenous immunoglobulin as first-line treatment for viral myocarditis
options.rationale: Intravenous immunoglobulin has not been shown to improve outcomes in adult viral myocarditis and is not recommended as routine therapy. Management focuses on supportive care including heart failure therapy and monitoring for complications. Immunosuppression may be considered in specific subtypes such as giant cell myocarditis or eosinophilic myocarditis. (Source: UpToDate)

options.text: High-dose prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for presumed immune-mediated myocarditis
options.rationale: Empiric immunosuppression is not recommended for acute viral myocarditis and may worsen viral replication. Corticosteroids have a role in specific subtypes such as giant cell myocarditis, cardiac sarcoidosis, or eosinophilic myocarditis, but these require endomyocardial biopsy for confirmation before commencing immunosuppressive therapy. (Source: UpToDate)

explanation.summary: This young man presents with acute myocarditis following a viral prodrome. Key discriminators from pericarditis include significant troponin elevation, reduced LVEF with global hypokinesis, elevated NT-proBNP, and signs of heart failure (elevated JVP, third heart sound, crepitations). Management priorities are guideline-directed heart failure therapy with an ACE inhibitor and cautious beta-blocker introduction, strict exercise restriction for 3 to 6 months, and continuous cardiac monitoring for arrhythmias. Cardiac MRI is the gold standard non-invasive investigation, demonstrating myocardial oedema on T2-weighted sequences and late gadolinium enhancement. NSAIDs are contraindicated in the presence of LV dysfunction.
key_points: Myocarditis is distinguished from pericarditis by significant troponin elevation, reduced LV function, and clinical heart failure
NSAIDs are contraindicated in myocarditis with LV dysfunction due to risk of worsening heart failure
Exercise restriction for 3 to 6 months is essential due to arrhythmia risk during the acute and recovery phases
explanation.pearls: Cardiac MRI confirms myocarditis using the updated Lake Louise criteria: T2-weighted sequences show myocardial oedema (acute inflammation) and late gadolinium enhancement shows myocardial necrosis or fibrosis. Exercise restriction should continue for 3 to 6 months with repeat imaging and normalisation of troponin and inflammatory markers before return to activity.

stem: A 62-year-old man with a history of chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 150 micromol/L) and non-adherence to antihypertensives presents to the emergency department with acute dyspnoea and orthopnoea that developed over 3 hours. On examination he is diaphoretic and in severe respiratory distress. Chest auscultation reveals bilateral crepitations to the mid-zones. There is a laterally displaced apex beat. Chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary oedema with an enlarged cardiac silhouette.

data_table.Vitals: Heart rate: 118 bpm; Blood pressure: 248/136 mmHg; MAP: 173 mmHg; Respiratory rate: 32 breaths/min; SpO2: 86% on 15 L/min non-rebreather mask
data_table.Investigations: Creatinine: 280 micromol/L (baseline 150); Potassium: 5.8 mmol/L; Troponin T: 56 ng/L (reference < 14); NT-proBNP: 8400 pg/mL; ABG pH: 7.28; ABG pCO2: 32 mmHg; ABG pO2: 54 mmHg on 15 L/min

lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial antihypertensive management?

options.text: Sodium nitroprusside infusion at 0.3 mcg/kg/min titrated to reduce MAP by 25% over the first hour
options.rationale: Sodium nitroprusside is a potent arterial and venous vasodilator but carries a risk of cyanide toxicity, particularly in patients with renal impairment as this patient has. It also requires invasive arterial blood pressure monitoring. Glyceryl trinitrate is preferred in hypertensive emergency with acute pulmonary oedema as it provides venodilation to reduce preload and has a more favourable safety profile. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular)

options.text: Oral amlodipine 10 mg and ramipril 5 mg as first-line agents for blood pressure reduction
options.rationale: Oral antihypertensive agents are not appropriate for hypertensive emergencies with acute target organ damage. They have unpredictable absorption and onset, making titration to blood pressure targets unreliable. This patient has acute pulmonary oedema, acute kidney injury, and hypoxaemic respiratory failure requiring intravenous therapy with careful blood pressure titration. ACE inhibitors are also relatively contraindicated acutely with hyperkalaemia and AKI. (Source: NHFA)

options.text: Labetalol 20 mg IV bolus repeated every 10 minutes up to 300 mg with continuous arterial monitoring
options.rationale: Labetalol is an appropriate intravenous agent for many hypertensive emergencies but is not the preferred choice when acute pulmonary oedema is the predominant target organ damage. Its negative inotropic effects from beta-blockade may worsen cardiac output in acute decompensated heart failure. Glyceryl trinitrate infusion is preferred as it reduces preload through venodilation and improves pulmonary congestion. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular)

options.text: Enalaprilat 1.25 mg IV to address the underlying cardiac afterload as first-line therapy
options.rationale: Intravenous enalaprilat is not widely available in Australia and is not first-line for hypertensive emergency with pulmonary oedema. In this patient, ACE inhibition is relatively contraindicated acutely due to the acute kidney injury (creatinine doubled from baseline) and hyperkalaemia (5.8 mmol/L), which may be worsened by renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system blockade. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Glyceryl trinitrate infusion at 5 mcg/min titrated to reduce MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour
options.rationale: This patient has a hypertensive emergency with acute pulmonary oedema, AKI, and hypoxaemic respiratory failure. Glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) infusion is the preferred first-line agent for hypertensive emergency complicated by acute pulmonary oedema. It acts primarily as a venodilator, reducing preload and pulmonary congestion, with arterial vasodilation at higher doses. The target is to reduce MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour to avoid precipitating cerebral or coronary hypoperfusion. Concurrent management should include high-dose IV furosemide and consideration of non-invasive ventilation for respiratory support. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: This patient presents with a hypertensive emergency defined by severely elevated blood pressure with acute target organ damage - in this case acute pulmonary oedema, acute kidney injury, and myocardial injury (elevated troponin). This is distinct from hypertensive urgency where blood pressure is severely elevated but without target organ damage. For hypertensive emergency with acute pulmonary oedema, glyceryl trinitrate infusion is the preferred agent as it reduces preload and pulmonary congestion through venodilation. Blood pressure should be reduced by no more than 25% of MAP in the first hour to prevent organ hypoperfusion, then gradually to 160/100 mmHg over the next 2 to 6 hours. Sodium nitroprusside carries cyanide toxicity risk, particularly with renal impairment.
key_points: Hypertensive emergency requires IV antihypertensive therapy; oral agents are inappropriate due to unpredictable onset and inability to titrate
GTN infusion is preferred for hypertensive emergency with acute pulmonary oedema due to its preload-reducing venodilatory effects
Target MAP reduction is no more than 25% in the first hour to avoid cerebral and coronary hypoperfusion
explanation.pearls: Glyceryl trinitrate infusion is commenced at 5 mcg/min and titrated every 5 minutes. Sodium nitroprusside should be avoided in renal impairment due to accumulation of the cyanide metabolite thiocyanate. In hypertensive emergency, the distinction between emergency (target organ damage present) and urgency (no target organ damage) determines whether IV or oral therapy is appropriate.
